Home › The Davisson-Germer Experiment

The Davisson-Germer Experiment

The Davisson-Germer Experiment (1927) provided the first experimental confirmation of the de Broglie Hypothesis, proving that electrons behave as waves. In university-level physics, this experiment is the "Mirror" of X-ray diffraction: it showed that a beam of particles reflecting off a crystal surface produces an interference pattern, definitively establishing Wave-Particle Duality as a physical reality.

1. The Setup: Electron Scattering

Clinton Davisson and Lester Germer fired low-energy electrons at a nickel crystal target in a vacuum. They measured the intensity of scattered electrons as a function of the scattering angle (\( \phi \)). According to classical physics, the intensity should be uniform. Instead, they found sharp "peaks" of intensity at specific angles, exactly as one would see with Electromagnetic Radiation.

2. Application of Bragg's Law

The peaks intensity were the result of Interference between matter waves reflecting off different planes of atoms in the crystal. This described by Bragg's Law: \[ n\lambda = d \sin heta \] By calculating the Matter Wave wavelength from the accelerating voltage (\( V \)) and the de Broglie Relation, Davisson and Germer found that the measured diffraction angles matched theory to within 1%. This was the "Smoking Gun" for quantum mechanics.

3. The Nickel Crystal Accident

The discovery was partly accidental. During the experiment, a vacuum leak caused the nickel target to oxidize. To clean it, Davisson and Germer heated the nickel, which caused it to form large, highly regular crystal grains. These grains acted as a Diffraction Grating for the electrons. This "Clean Lab" accident revealed the hidden Geometric Origin of the electron's behavior, which had been blurred in poly-crystalline samples.

4. Matter Wave vs. X-ray Distinction

A university-level detail is the distinction between electron diffraction and X-ray diffraction. While X-rays interact with the electron cloud of atoms, electrons interact with the Electric Potential (nucleus and cloud). This makes electron diffraction much more sensitive to the surface properties of materials, leading to the modern technology of LEED (Low-Energy Electron Diffraction).

5. Confirmation of non-relativistic c

The experiment also verified that the wavelength follows the non-relativistic momentum formula at low speeds. For a voltage \( V \), the wavelength is \( \lambda = h/\sqrt{2meV} \). The success of this formula across different voltages proved that the Planck Constant (\( h \)) is the universal scaling factor between the classical world of energy and the quantum world of waves.

6. Legacy: The Birth of Quantum Engineering

The Davisson-Germer experiment turned "Matter Waves" from a wild hypothesis into a practical tool. It led directly to the development of the Electron Microscope, which uses the short wavelengths of high-speed electrons to see objects thousands of times smaller than light microscopes can. It proved that the Total Dynamics of the subatomic world can be controlled by manipulating the wave phase of matter.

Key Theoretical Identities

Interact with identities to explore their semantic depth.

  • Interference Proof [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ n\lambda = d \sin \phi \quad ( ext{Diffraction Peak}) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Interference Proof: The observation that electrons only bounce into specific spots, like waves.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Crystal math [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ \lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_e e V}} \approx \frac{12.27}{\sqrt{V}} ext{ \AA} \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Crystal math: The law that relates atomic spacing to the "angle of sparkle" for light and matter.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Speed-Color Link [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ E = eV \quad ( ext{Accelerating Energy}) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Speed-Color Link: How changing the electric push changes the electron's wave color.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Surface Probe [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ I( heta) \propto | \sum e^{i \Delta \Phi} |^2 \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Surface Probe: Why electron waves are the best way to map the top layer of a material.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.
